confidence.disclosure: Route reconstructed from the ten-volume Seyahatname itself, as edited and translated by Robert Dankoff and Sooyong Kim. Volume sequence and major dates are securely documented; some specific years for stops within longer campaigns are approximate. A handful of episodes in Books 6 and 7 (notably his claimed visit to the Netherlands) are regarded by scholars as fictional and have been omitted.

sources: Robert Dankoff and Sooyong Kim, An Ottoman Traveller: Selections from the Book of Travels of Evliya Çelebi (2010)
Robert Dankoff, An Ottoman Mentality: The World of Evliya Çelebi (2004)

journey.event: Born in Istanbul to a wealthy goldsmith of the Ottoman court.
First independent journey, slipping out of Istanbul without his father's permission to visit Bursa.
Travelled around the Sea of Marmara region recorded in Book 2.
Travelled to the Black Sea coast as part of Ketenci Ömer Pasha's retinue.
Visited the Crimean Khanate, recording the Tatar court of the Giray dynasty.
Joined the Ottoman expeditionary force during the early phase of the Cretan War.
Served as muezzin and companion to Defterdarzade Mehmed Pasha, governor of Erzurum.
Travelled with Murtaza Pasha through Syria, recording Damascus in detail in Book 3.
Visited the Haram al-Sharif and the Christian holy sites, knowledge he later used to charm Emperor Leopold I.
Recorded the Anatolian heartland in Book 3.
Accompanied Melek Ahmed Pasha to his governorship of Van, on the eastern Anatolian frontier.
Stayed as guest of Abdal Khan, recording the Kurdish emirate's palace and library in detail.
Recorded the Tigris-bank fortress city and its bazaars.
Crossed into Safavid territory on a diplomatic mission, recorded in Book 4.
Visited the oil fields and fire-temples of Baku on the Caspian shore.
Visited the Abbasid capital and recorded its monuments in Book 4.
Served Melek Ahmed Pasha during his governorship of Rumelia from Sofia, recorded in Book 5.
Travelled through Ottoman Bosnia, recording the city's bridges, mosques and dervish lodges.
Joined the Ottoman army marching against the Habsburgs in the war of 1663-64.
Eyewitness to the Battle of St. Gotthard, where Ottoman forces were defeated by Imperial troops.
Joined the Ottoman embassy under Kara Mehmed Pasha; received audience with Emperor Leopold I.
Returned to the Crimean Khanate before journeying north and east.
Reached the Volga delta and recorded the Kalmyk and Russian populations in Book 7.
Returned south via Azov to Ottoman territory after his Caucasus and Volga journeys.
Toured Ottoman Greece, leaving an early Ottoman description of the Acropolis and the Parthenon.
Eyewitness to the final Ottoman conquest of Candia, ending the 21-year siege.
Travelled through Ottoman Albania before returning to Istanbul.
Set out from Istanbul on the Hajj via his ancestral Kütahya, recorded in Book 9.
Recorded Smyrna, including its European Frankish merchant quarter.
Joined the Damascus Hajj caravan after passing through Aleppo.
Visited the tomb of the Prophet Muhammad as part of his Hajj.
Performed the Hajj, fulfilling the pilgrimage that closes Book 9.
Settled in Cairo, his final base, where he composed Book 10 of the Seyahatname.
Toured the Nile delta from Cairo, recording Alexandria, Rosetta and Damietta.
Travelled up the Nile to the southern frontier of Ottoman Egypt.
Reached the Funj Sultanate of Sennar on the Blue Nile during his Sudan-Ethiopia safari.
Visited the Red Sea port of Suakin and recorded the trade with the Hijaz.
Died in Cairo around 1682-1684 after completing the Seyahatname.
